data.title: Janus Henderson Intermediate Term Income Managed Account Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
At a glance

Performance

The Portfolio returned 1.21% (gross) and the Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index returned 1.10%.

Contributors/detractors

Overall yield curve positioning contributed, while our spread risk allocation detracted.

Outlook

A resilient economy, artificial intelligence (AI)-driven investment, and anticipated Fed easing create a favorable backdrop for fixed income in 2026. Active, diversified strategies focused on carry and risk-adjusted returns remain essential, in our view.

Investment environment

  • The U.S. fixed income market delivered strong positive quarterly returns, capping off the best calendar year of performance since 2020. Short-term Treasury yields rallied, helping to drive positive returns and a steeper yield curve. High yield outperformed U.S. Treasuries and investment-grade (IG) corporates.
  • In December, the Federal Reserve (Fed) cut interest rates by 25 basis points (bps) for a third consecutive meeting in response to a weaker jobs market, with the U.S. unemployment rate hitting a four-year high of 4.6% in November. Other data releases were more positive, with inflation dipping below expectations in November and third-quarter gross domestic product (GDP) growth that surprised on the upside.
  • Despite policymakers indicating that they anticipate one more cut in 2026, futures markets are pricing in an additional two to three cuts in 2026. Speculation also continued to build around who would replace Fed Chair Jerome Powell when his term expires in May, and the degree to which their appointment might signal a shift in the central bank's traditional operational independence.
  • The U.S. 10-year Treasury yield ended the quarter two bps higher, at 4.17%. IG corporate spreads widened four bps, to 78 bps, while high-yield spreads were virtually unchanged, at 266 bps, as investor sentiment remained upbeat.

Portfolio review

Our positioning on the short end of the yield curve contributed as the curve steepened, with short-term Treasury yields rallying while long-term yields remained rangebound. We continue to favor an overweight to the short end of the yield curve as a hedge against any potential weakening in the economy or a rise in term premiums due to potential concerns surrounding Fed independence, large and continued fiscal deficit spending, and tariff effects, with the Fed seemingly willing to let inflation run higher than target. We believe this view is supported by the combination of forthcoming rate cuts, tax cuts, stubborn inflation, and upward revisions to growth estimates.

Our allocation to credit-spread risk detracted. Specific detractors included our allocation to mortgage-backed securities and exposure to the banking and home construction sectors. Allocations to high-yield corporates, non-agency mortgages, and commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS) contributed.

We maintained our overweight allocation to high yield on the favorable economic outlook and supply dynamics, as well as to securitized sectors, where valuations continue to look attractive on a relative basis. We maintained our thematic tilts in technology, gaming, midstream, utilities, and in AI exposure via asset-backed securities and CMBS data-center deals.

Manager outlook

2025 was an exceptional year for fixed income and we are also constructive on the outlook for 2026, albeit with a focus on earning carry rather than pursuing significant spread compression. In our view, current yield levels continue to present an attractive entry point for multi-sector fixed income strategies, supported by a constructive macroeconomic backdrop and favorable monetary policy trends.

U.S. economic growth is expected to remain positive, with Fed real GDP projections at over 2.0% for 2026. While some sectors exhibit late-cycle characteristics, resilient consumer spending and corporate profitability – bolstered by AI adoption – should sustain economic momentum. Significant AI-driven investments in chips and data centers are anticipated to further fuel growth.

We think risks surrounding tariffs, immigration reform, and geopolitics are expected to moderate, while tax reforms under the One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA) may provide additional tailwinds. Inflation, despite disinflationary progress in energy and housing, is likely to remain above the Fed's target, in the 2.6% to 3.0% range, due to sticky services prices and delayed tariff effects.

We believe positioning on the short end of the yield curve may benefit from expected steepening and mitigate risks from rising term premiums. Fiscal deficits and concerns over Fed independence may keep long-end yields rangebound. While uncertainty surrounds the Fed's near-term decisions, markets anticipate two to three rate cuts by year-end 2026. The Fed's pivot from quantitative tightening to quantitative easing – and its readiness to ease aggressively if growth falters – should support spreads. While AI-driven productivity gains and tighter immigration policies could create a unique dynamic of strong growth alongside weaker labor markets, reinforcing the case for continued Fed easing.

Broadly speaking, key themes within the portfolio include our bullishness on credit spread carry amid robust corporate fundamentals and expected Fed cuts. Furthermore, real yields are still above 1% and offer inflation-adjusted return potential. Finally, attractive valuations and strong credit quality within securitized assets relative to corporates support our overweight to securitized sectors.

A resilient economy, AI-driven investment, and anticipated Fed easing create a favorable backdrop for fixed income in 2026. Active, diversified strategies focused on carry and risk-adjusted returns remain essential, in our view.

data.title: Blue Owl Capital: It's Darkest Before The Dawn
data.description:
Investment Rating - "Buy"

Blue Owl Capital Corporation (OBDC) is currently the second-largest business development company (BDC) in the United States by total assets, according to the firm's investor relations data. The most recent IR update shows OBDC's portfolio size is ~$16.5 billion, spread across 234 companies in 30 portfolio industries:

Like most of its peers, OBDC's net asset values have been in steady decline since mid-2024 as expectations for rate cuts increased amid the cool-off in forward inflation rates. Given that over 96% of OBDC's debt portfolio was in floating-rate loans, the rate cutting directly compressed asset yields, leading to lower net income from the portfolio assets. Investors started to question the sustainability of OBDC's generous dividend yield, which is why it's currently standing at the highest average level since at least 2019, according to Seeking Alpha (the higher the yield, the riskier the investment in the BDC world):

In addition to that, there were some credit-related markdowns and unrealized depreciation on a small number of watch-list names, as the management noted, which caused OBDC's NAV reduction in the latest quarters. If we add the recent rise in redemption requests at some non-public BDCs to the equation, we'll get a perfect storm for OBDC - that's why the stock is down so much, and it keeps falling lower.

However, I think the saying that "it's usually darkest before the dawn" fits well in this case. In my opinion, OBDC's portfolio looks good in general, and its NAV reduction lately doesn't seem to pose a massive risk to this BDC's investors - the recent institutional asset sale at near par proves that. Also, I think that the rise in inflation expectations that we got following the US-Iran conflict and higher oil prices suggests that the market's prevailing assumption of OBDC's collapse in NII and potential cuts in dividend payouts has been broken - I'll explain why in the next chapters.

At today's price levels, OBDC already looks attractive, so I'm rating it as a "Buy" BDC investment for the long run.

Higher-For-Longer Rates Is A Plus For OBDC

As I noted above, the vast majority of OBDC's debt portfolio is structured with floating interest rates, which practically means that when the Fed starts to cut, OBDC's net investment income (NII) gets hit hard. Due to spreads and operating leverage, the net effect might be quite significant, in a bad sense. In Q4 2025, for example, we saw an adjusted NII of $0.36 per share, which was 23.4% below last year's level, and it was even lower than the quarterly dividend payout.

The difference between Q4 NII and the quarterly payout was only 1 cent, or 2.7%, but it was enough for the market to significantly reprice the stock to the downside, likely pricing in upcoming dividend cuts. However, I wouldn't be so categorical here.

We've seen some resilience in the US economy lately. But with the labor markets stabilizing and services inflation remaining persistently sticky, I believe the recent repricing in the Fed's cutting odds are pointing to higher-for-longer rates. The market's environment looks "all inflationary," as JPMorgan's (JPM) CEO said recently, especially so amid the rise in oil prices following the conflict in the Middle East.

In this type of market environment, it's hard to envision rate cuts - if the Fed cuts, the risk of inflation going out of control would rise dramatically, just like we saw in the first post-COVID years.

So my guess is that the rising risk-free rates, like 10-year note yields, and hence the lower odds of the Fed's cutting cycle, are providing a bullish narrative shift for OBDC, as most of its debt portfolio has floating rates. The floating rates are tied to benchmarks like SOFR, which follows the effective federal funds rate - as long as there are no cuts, SOFR should bottom out and stop pressing OBDC's NII, in my understanding. Thus, it effectively de-risks the BDC's dividend payments and saves time for future capital reallocation.

If so, the market's expectations that OBDC will ultimately cut payout amounts in the next 2 years seem to have gone too far:

Of course, there's a risk of some cutting, but I'm not certain that it's to the extent the Wall Street analysts price in to date.

Another risk that gets priced in is the NAC erosion, and it partially explains the current discount to NAV. But as Goldman Sachs analysts showed in their research note, OBDC's NAV trajectory is one of the most stable ones in the peer group:

There was some unrealized depreciation on a small, isolated number of watchlist names within the portfolio, but in general, the portfolio companies keep growing their EBITDA figures (see the first chart at the very beginning of this article). Also, the management is running an aggressive share repurchase program - in Q4 alone, OBDC bought back ~$148 million worth of stock from the market at ~86% of price-to-book value, thus adding ~$0.05/share to the firm's NAV, which is great, in my opinion.

OBDC sold $1.4 billion of its direct lending investments - those that the market seems to consider toxic in recent months - at about 99.7% of par value, following intense third-party due diligence, according to the firm. It actually directly speaks to the quality of their debt portfolio, proving that the fears are likely overblown right now.

I liked OBDC's focus on specialty finance and joint ventures, which is only 12% of the whole portfolio as of Q4, but this share is set to rise further as we go into 2027-2028.

Across all our specialty finance and joint ventures, OBDC's exposure is approximately 12%, providing us with ample opportunity to selectively increase our allocation as market conditions warrant. To close, the breadth and strength of our portfolio remains resilient in a shifting and more recently uncertain market backdrop. With 10 years of operating history and an even longer tenure of experienced professionals underwriting and managing the book, we are seeing durable fundamental performance of our borrowers, and we remain convicted in our diversified lending strategy.

OBDC's Q4 earnings call

Once this area gains a bigger share within the revenue base, I think even when rates drop, OBDC's total portfolio will be safer than it's currently perceived by most analysts - it sounds like a solid reason for a future bullish repricing to me.

OBDC's Undervaluation Setup

In Q4 2025, OBDC reported its NAV per share at $14.81, and it was only slightly below the Q4 2025 level:

Today's OBDC stock price is $11.32/share, so the discount to NAV right now stands at ~23.56%. According to Morningstar's analysts, "Over the past 20 years, the typical BDC traded at a discount of roughly 6.6% to its NAV." However, they continue, "When the average BDC traded below a 20% discount, it typically quickly recovered."

From this, I can conclude that to justify its current discount to NAV, one should assume that OBDC is on the verge of systematic credit defaults across its entire portfolio, and it's not matching reality. In Q4 2025, we saw that non-accruals sit at a microscopic 1.1% of fair value - it doesn't look catastrophic at all if you ask me.

Ares Capital (ARCC) trades at a discount to NAV of only ~8.32%, but I wouldn't assume that OBDC should narrow this massive gap - Ares is clearly better capitalized and less risky, given its portfolio structure and recent financials (their NAV is growing, not contracting). But I think OBDC's fair discount should be lower than 23-24% - something within the range of 15-18% would look reasonable, given my previous assumptions regarding the potential bullish repricing of its floating-rate-dependent portfolio and related risks.

Assuming that OBDC closes FY2026 with a NAV of $14.75 (6 cents below the Q4 level) and the discount shrinks to the desired 15-18% range, the resulting fair value per share comes in at $12.32. That is 8.80% above today's price. On top of that, we should also add the FY2026 dividend yield of 12.67%, based on Seeking Alpha data, and we'll get the total return potential of almost 22%.

Risks To My Views

The main risk to my thesis lies in the BDC's high sensitivity to falling interest rates and tighter spreads, as well as the continued rise in redemption requests. It's hard to predict exactly how the Fed will act going forward and for how long the current geopolitical instability will prevail so that oil prices remain as high as they are today. If the rising inflation risks are overblown and the Fed cuts, SOFR will logically follow the effective federal funds rate, creating pressure on OBDC's NII throughout 2026-2027. So today's priced-in consensus dividend payouts that I don't agree with might actually turn out to be right.

On top of that, we see the risks of sector-specific disruption in areas where OBDC's portfolio holds some exposure. The concentration in SaaS-related industries right now doesn't look excessive (11-13%), but if these companies get disrupted by AI and they stop paying interest, OBDC's NII can drop unexpectedly, justifying today's large discount to NAV.

Conclusion

OBDC has been contracting for a good reason, and its current discount to NAV might actually look fair if we assume the above risk factors are core assumptions for future periods. However, I think that the sell-off has already gone too far, and it's just overdone, given the rising odds of the Fed's pause and the overall OBDC's portfolio quality. Their average company keeps scaling on the EBITDA level, and the default risks seem to be minimal right now. I believe that OBDC can reprice higher from here; my calculations point to a highly likely total return potential of almost 22% to the current stock price.

data.title: EPS: WisdomTree's Earnings-Based Strategy Offers Limited Upside Potential Vs. SPY
data.description:
Investment Thesis

This article continues my coverage of the WisdomTree U.S. LargeCap ETF (EPS), which I last reviewed on March 28, 2023. At the time, I found its fundamentals were extremely similar to the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY), so I decided there wasn't a serious opportunity to buy this lesser-known, earnings-based ETF. Since that review was published, EPS has delivered a 67.64% total return, which was about 3% lower than SPY's 70.38% gain.

These returns align with EPS's performance since its February 2007 inception, and after reviewing EPS's latest fundamentals and factor mix, I found that there may be a small value opportunity today based on its P/E ratios, which are about 12% discounted to SPY's. Still, I want to emphasize that any outperformance will likely be small and likely short-lived, and as such, it's not enough to move off my prior "hold" rating for EPS.

EPS Overview and Strategy Summary

EPS, launched on February 23, 2007, has a 0.08% expense ratio, and tracks the WisdomTree U.S. LargeCap Index, which, according to WisdomTree, "is a fundamentally weighted index that measures the performance of earnings-generating companies within the large-capitalization segment of the U.S. Stock Market." Per this presentation, WisdomTree's philosophy is that market-cap-weighting schemes are flawed, pricing errors and return premiums exist, and fundamentals and the concept of relative value are important, which is why the first screen applied is for companies with positive earnings over the last four quarters. Furthermore, WisdomTree applies a composite risk scoring system across two universes (large/mid-caps and small-caps) and removes the bottom 10%, effectively rewarding high-quality companies with positive risk-adjusted momentum.

The 500 largest stocks are selected for inclusion, with weightings based on earnings rather than market capitalization. This step gives EPS its value lean, and on the surface, this approach sounds excellent. However, readers should know that:

  • The S&P 500 Index Committee also considers profitability.
  • The largest U.S. stocks are also among the most profitable.
  • EPS and SPY overlap by 79.50%.

The overlap is important, as it translates to only 20.50% active share, which isn't big enough to drive meaningful excess returns. Furthermore, three quarters (~15%) can be explained by 20 stocks. Starting with the EPS's top ten overweights, the following stocks comprise 7.02% more in EPS than they do in SPY, and in aggregate, they trade at 19.99x trailing earnings.

In contrast, EPS underweights the following ten stocks by a combined 7.49%, which, in aggregate, trade at 38.93x trailing earnings. This group includes Apple (AAPL), Tesla (TSLA), and Broadcom (AVGO), all of which are trillion-dollar companies. As a result, EPS has a slight size factor advantage over SPY, which may be beneficial if markets broaden.

Still, the takeaway for readers is that EPS's approach isn't very different from SPY's. I suppose the concept made more sense in 2007, when the tech crash in the early 2000s was still relatively fresh. A strategy that would have avoided low-quality stocks with high market caps would be welcome, but that's not applicable in today's environment. Using two of WisdomTree's own measures of quality, seven of SPY's top ten holdings have an ROA and ROE above the Index's averages of 14.87% and 29.12%. Therefore, underweighting them in the name of attaining a slightly cheaper P/E is a questionable strategy.

EPS Performance Snapshot

These strategy similarities and low active share have translated to similar returns since EPS's inception. As shown below, EPS has gained an annualized 9.87% since March 2007 compared to 10.35%, with volatility (standard deviation), drawdowns, best and worst twelve-month returns, and risk-adjusted returns (Sharpe and Sortino ratios) being nearly identical. SPY has the advantage in most cases, and the calendar-year returns table indicates most is traced to 2020, an obvious outlier year due to the COVID-19 pandemic. For many years, EPS and SPY have been within a couple of percentage points of each other.

This observation is supported by the following returns distribution analysis. You'll notice how both ETFs have similar distributions, with the main criticism of EPS being a slightly higher frequency (25.3% vs. 22.7%) of returns in the -5% to 0% bucket. Its median monthly return was also slightly below SPY's (1.46% vs. 1.51%), and since this statistic lessens the impact of outlier events, it gives investors an idea of what kinds of returns they can expect over the long run. Unfortunately, it's not in EPS's favor.

EPS Composition and Fundamental Analysis

From a sector perspective relative to SPY, EPS overweights Financials and Communication Services by 3.58% and 2.55%, and underweights Technology and Industrials by 3.07% and 2.78%. Again, this active sector risk isn't much, but it does result in a slight value lean. For example, The Financials Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLF) trades at 16.43x trailing earnings, while Technology's (XLK) trailing P/E is much higher at 32.43x.

However, this setup doesn't necessarily mean EPS is less risky. Earnings variability for Technology and Financials has been quite similar over the last decade, and even though XLF's drawdowns have generally been lower than XLK's, its recovery times were often longer. For example, it took 16 months for XLF to recover from its 23.13% decline from November 2021 to September 2022, whereas XLK recovered from its deeper 31.22% drawdown in just 9 months. As a result, technology was the better play from peak to trough, which I think speaks to the sector's higher quality.

Lastly, please consider the following fundamental statistics for EPS's top ten holdings, which comprise 38.51% of the portfolio. As I'm sure you've guessed, this concentration level is close to SPY's 38.60%, and after reviewing the table below, I'm sure you'll agree that the two funds are quite similar.

Based on the high-level statistics at the bottom, EPS and SPY have similar five-year portfolio betas (1.09 vs. 1.13), while SPY retains a slight advantage on margins (EBIT and net) and capital efficiency (return on total capital). However, EPS's 22.82x and 18.48x trailing and forward P/E ratios represent about a 12% discount to SPY, which I expect will be a source of near-term outperformance. I base this expectation on decelerating S&P 500 Index earnings surprises, which registered at 5.6% in Q4 2025, and although one quarter is not a trend, the last time earnings surprises decelerated was from Q2 to Q4 of 2021. For me, this signals risk-off sentiment, and like in 2021-2022, 2018, and 2007-2008, that should benefit value stocks.

Investment Recommendation

EPS is a well-established large-cap blend fund with a 0.08% expense ratio and $1.3B in assets under management. WisdomTree's pitch is that cap-weighting schemes used for ETFs like SPY are inherently flawed and that its earnings-based approach that focuses on the concept of relative value is superior. While this strategy sounds reasonable, it overlooks the current composition of the U.S. stock market and ends up underweighting several high-quality stocks, including Nvidia and Apple. Furthermore, EPS overlaps with SPY by 80%, so all EPS offers is an SPY-like portfolio with a very small value lean. This explains why the two ETFs deliver near-identical returns in most years, and while I expect a rotation to value will benefit EPS, the impact will be minimal. As a result, a "hold" rating for EPS is most appropriate. Thank you for reading.

data.title: Miami International Holdings: Strong Growth, But Earnings Quality Raises Questions
data.description:
Miami International Holdings, Inc. (MIAX), is showing fast revenue growth, though it remains unprofitable by the GAAP standards. In 2025, the company's revenue reached $430.5 million, which is 56% more than in 2024, though the yearly GAAP net loss was $70 million, compared to last year's $102.1 million in profits. This negative result was caused by $107.7 million in debt settlement costs and $54.9 million in unrealized losses from digital asset management.

Despite that, operational activities show economies of scale: adjusted EBITDA in 2025 grew 143%, while margins expanded from 30% to 46%. The main business engine remains the options segment, whose market share in 2025 Q4 reached a record level of 18.2%, though in February 2026 it was only 17.1%. The forward P/E is currently at around 27.5x, based on forecasted $1.65 earnings per share.

This suggests that investors value the company as a growth asset, focusing on the $1.82 adjusted EPS rather than the accounting loss. The strategic 90% sale of MIAXdx shares to the Robinhood consortium and the International Stock Exchange acquisition show a goal to diversify income outside the US, even though the futures segment is still generating operational losses. In 2026, the company is forecasting adjusted operational costs to be between $265-$275 million, which signals further investments in technological infrastructure.

Business overview

Miami International Holdings, Inc.'s business model is based on four US options exchanges, a stock platform, MIAX Pearl Equities, and futures exchange market MGEX management, generating income from execution of trades, market data sales, as well as technological access fees. Current data shows that the company is successfully carrying out product rotation, seeking to lower dependency on the US option exchange market, which still represents a big market share of 18.2%.

Even though options in 2025 made up the biggest part of the whole revenue of $430.5 million, the MIAX Pearl Equities segment in 2026 reached 1.7% US stock market share, processing 1.4 billion shares in a month, which shows a stable expansion to a very competitive segment. A strategic turn happened in January 2026 when MIAX sold 90% of shares to Robinhood, effectively leaving the direct digital asset exchange management and choosing to become an infrastructure provider.

This step, together with the acquisition of The International Stock Exchange, shows capital rotation from risky cryptocurrency operations to a regulated international securities listing market, trying to attract European issuers. The futures exchange segment through MGEX in 2026 reported 2.4% volume growth, reaching over 315 thousand contracts, though this sector is still working at an operational loss, which shows that product diversification has yet to compensate for the high technological costs. The newest launch of the MIAX Sapphire exchange at the end of 2025 added a segment of physical trading floors, though it increased operational costs for 2026, with the forecast being at $275 million, which raises doubt about the achievement of quick net profit without further market share growth.

Valuation

Miami International Holdings valuation in 2026 reflects the market's expectations for further aggressive expansion, though numbers are showing a high valuation premium compared to the sector's old-timers. Share price is around $39.0, which is 70% more than the 2025 IPO price of $23.0. Fiscal year 2025 results showed a negative GAAP EPS of $-1.00, due to IPO and expansion costs. Adjusted earnings per share reached $1.82, and the current adjusted P/E ratio is at 21.5x.

Though looking at 2026 forecasts, where EPS is expected to be at $1.65, and forward P/E is reaching around 27.5x. This is much higher than the main competitor, Cboe Global Markets (CBOE), whose historical P/E ratio is around 20-22x. MIAX's valuation is based on rapid revenue growth in 2025, which reached 56% at $430.5 million, and an expanded adjusted EBITDA margin, which reached 46%.

However, a P/S rate over 8.4 signals a tense valuation, especially knowing that MIAX's market share in the stock segment in 2026 shrunk to 0.9%, while future growth depends on the successful integration of new products, such as Bloomberg Index Futures. Risk is also increased because of the expected operational cost growth in 2026, which could limit further margin expansion, so the current price leaves no room for operational mistakes.

Risks

MIAX's financial state in 2026 is showing a clear gap between adjusted profitability and factual GAAP results, which creates a risk to the profit stability for investors. Even though in 2025 adjusted EPS reached $1.82, the company recorded a $70 million net loss by the standards of GAAP, which resulted in a $-1.00 EPS.

This loss was mostly generated by one-time costs: a $107.7 million cost on debt settlement and a $54.9 million unrealized loss on derivatives. Such fluctuation shows that net results are very dependent on non-operational factors, which can distort the true value of the business. Also, it is forecasted that operational costs in 2026 will grow, which puts pressure on margins if trading volumes slow down in the market.

Although the option trading market share at the end of 2025 Q4 reached a record level of 18.2%, the company remains very dependent on this one segment. The futures trades segment in 2025 reported an operational loss of $14.2 million, while the stock trading market share in 2026 dropped to 0.9%, compared to 1.1% last year. This suggests an inability to effectively diversify income streams outside of the core business. At a current 27.50 forward P/E, the market is expecting aggressive growth, though any regulatory change, for example, the proposed reduction in the Options Regulatory Fee (ORF) from $0.0016 to $0.0013 per contract, could directly lower revenues. Finally, the 90% MIAIdx share sale to Robinhood limits direct MIAIX participation in the growth of the digital asset market, leaving only a 10% part and lowering long term potential for independent business.

Thesis summary

In conclusion, Miami International Holdings is currently in a transitional phase between a growth story and a real, stable business model. On one side, numbers are showing clear operational progress, revenues are growing aggressively, EBITDA margins are expanding, and the options segment is further increasing market share. This shows that the core business is working and is even starting to generate a scale effect.

Though looking from another point of view, the stock is not as attractive. GAAP loss, even if partially explained by the one-off factors, shows that profitability is still not stable and heavily relies on non-operational elements. Also, the growing costs and weak progress in other segments, especially in the futures market, raise the question of whether diversification actually works or if it is still a one-product (options) story.

Valuing by the current 27.5x forward P/E, the market has already priced in an aggressive growth scenario. This means that there is practically no room for error; any slowing of growth, margin pressure, or regulatory change could have a disproportionately big negative impact on the share price.

In my view, MIAIX is not a bad business; quite the opposite, it's a growing and strategically changing business that is trying to move from trading venue to infrastructure provider. But at the current stage, the model has yet to be proven, with the inconsistent earnings quality and high market expectations. That is why, until there are clear signals that profitability will stabilize and diversification will start generating real returns, I would value this stock with caution.

data.title: Prospect Capital: The 8% Yielding Preferreds Are The Only Reasonable Prospect
data.description:
Prospect Capital's (PSEC) constant net asset value ("NAV") dip and history of dividend cuts have kept the common shares as an avoid, but the 5.35% Series A Fixed Rate Cumulative Preferreds (PSEC.PR.A) look more attractive at their current level on the back of a $1.3375 per share annual coupon that's driving a current yield of 8.07%. PSEC last paid a monthly cash dividend of $0.045 per share, kept unchanged from its prior distribution for a 21.6% dividend yield. While the 1,353 basis point spread between both securities would imply the commons offer the better option for income investors, PSEC does not adequately currently cover its distribution, and the BDC has a history of substantial dividend cuts.

The monthly payout is down from $0.06 per share in 2024 and from $0.083 per share in 2017. The commons will always provide a higher dividend than the preferreds, with this dynamic likely to remain unless the pending dividend cuts is as steep as 65%. While PSEC is unlikely to rightsize its dividend by such an amount, the common shares will almost always follow a dividend cut lower, which means capital loss for holders of PSEC at the current level. The BDC held 91 portfolio companies with an aggregate fair value of $6.5 billion as of the end of its second quarter and was last rated junk at "BB+" by S&P Global Ratings, with PIK forming the main culprit for what was a downgrade.

PSEC last reported its fiscal 2026 second-quarter earnings, ending December 31, 2025. This saw total investment income of $176 million, a dip of around 5% from its year-ago level to drive net investment income ("NII") that came in at $0.19 per share, around $90.88 million on a nominal basis. The 3-month aggregate of the ongoing monthly payout at $0.135 per share means PSEC is covering its dividend by 141% against its second-quarter unadjusted NII per share. PSEC spent around $26.7 million in coupon payments to its preferred stockholders during the quarter, which works out to be a cost of $0.057 in NII as the BDC includes its preferreds in total equity.

This would reduce the dividend coverage to just 98.8% as NII would drop to $0.133 per share. This means initial common share coverage as per unadjusted NII, should not be viewed alone. The BDC does not fully cover its dividend, but its coupon payments to preferred holders are fully covered. The yield of both securities is further deepened by credit quality issues in PSEC's portfolio that have led to a dip in NAV. I last covered the BDC in October with a Hold rating based on continued NAV declines. This erosion has carried on into the most recent quarter.

The 8% Yield From The Series A Preferreds In An Age Of Chaos

The BDC had 8.6% of total investment income as payment-in-kind ("PIK") for the trailing twelve months as of the end of the second quarter. This is high, with BDCs like Main Street Capital (MAIN) with a PIK income constituting 2.8% of total investment income through 2025. Further, nonaccruals as a percentage of its portfolio at fair value were 0.7%. This was unchanged from the first quarter but was up 30 basis points from 0.4% in the year-ago comp.

The BDC is issuing new shares despite a stock that has dipped by 68% over the last 5 years. PSEC reported a NAV of $2.96 billion, around $6.21 per share, as of the end of its second quarter. Hence, with PSEC swapping hands for $2.50, the BDC is trading at a 60% discount to NAV per share, or for 40 cents on the dollar. It's hard to overstate how steep this discount is versus the broader BDC universe. The discount would be lower after adjusting for preferred equity.

Bears would be right to flag that the continued issuance of new shares presents a significant barrier to healthy total returns for the common shares. PSEC reported a weighted-average shares of common stock outstanding of 472,257,137 as of the end of the second quarter, up 8% year-over-year, with the figure more broadly up around 20% over the last 3 years. The continued dip in book value underpins the bear case for the commons, as the ongoing discount will remain sticky on the back of the continued erosion of book value. This has become a negative cycle of decline, where PSEC's NAV dip leads to a constant discount to NAV as the market prices in the prospects of a further dip in NAV, which makes the ongoing selling of new shares proportionally more dilutive. The preferreds are trading at a 35% discount to their $25 per share redemption value.

Conclusion

PSEC's elevated PIK income, track record of dividend cuts, and current lack of coverage mean a position in the more than 20% yielding commons stands to realize capital loss and a possible dividend cut. The preferreds are markedly safer as their coupon is covered by NII, albeit backed by a BDC that saw nonaccruals rise by 30 basis points. I am rating both securities as a Hold.

data.title: As A Stock, Walmart Offers Poor Value While Kroger Is Priced At A Discount
data.description:
Thesis: Walmart (NASDAQ:WMT) is a well-run company but currently share prices are simply too high and are pricing in a lot of future growth, thus reducing upside. Kroger (NYSE:KR) remains the better bet owing to its more attractive PE and emphasis on consumer staples at a time when many are struggling with cost-of-living challenges.

There’s a lot to like with Walmart’s business model and emphasis on value. No doubt, Walmart is a well-managed company and the king of brick-and-mortar retail. However, the valuation remains too rich even after the recent cooldown. Right now, I believe Kroger offers better value and a product portfolio that pairs well with consumers under financial strain.

I last covered Walmart in mid-November (2025) and came away with a hold rating owing to the high valuation. Share prices have climbed more than 15% since then, but the now higher valuation strikes me as unsustainable, and thus I am downgrading my score to sell. I last rated Kroger in September as a buy. KR is up only about ~7.75% since then but I believe it has more upside than WMT while offerring a similar risk profile. Thus, I maintain my buy rating for KR.

WMT And KR Fundamentals And Valuation Considerations

Let’s call out the bull in the retail shop: WMT is expensive. There’s no doubt about that. The company’s TTM GAAP PE ratio is an eye-watering 43.60 while the non-GAAP PE is 44.91. KR, on the other hand, has a TTM non-GAAP PE ratio of 15.06. That said, Kroger’s TTM GAAP PE ratio is likewise very high at 47.53. This is out of the norm for Kroger, which historically has enjoyed much lower GAAP PE ratios. The GAAP PE ratio factors in a $2.5 billion impairment charge, which reduced EPS by $2.91. The impairment charge is a result of failed efforts to automate warehouses. As such, I am more focused on KR’s non-GAAP PE ratio, which offers decent value at ~15.

KR enjoys an exceptionally cheap Price/Sales ratio at just .32 (TTM) to WMT’s respectable 1.33. WMT saw YOY revenue growth of 4.73%, according to Seeking Alpha data. This is a solid number for a brick-and-mortar retailer. However, with a +40 PE ratio, I’d want to see growth in line with hot tech stocks. While Walmart is finding success in e-commerce, it’s still a far cry from being a growing tech company. KR’s YOY revenue growth was .35%, but this is due in large part to the fact that KR sold its Specialty Pharmacy Business, which previously generated about $2 billion in revenue per year. Low fuel prices had an impact as well.

Walmart’s net income margin of ~3% is also a far cry from tech sector profits. This profitability is pretty solid for retail and trump’s KR’s .69%. Once again, however, the impairment charge impacted the numbers. Kroger does enjoy a higher levered free cash flow margin at 2.32% to WMT’s 1.48%. Kroger’s gross profit margin is also healthy at 23.3%, right on the heels of Walmart’s 24.93% gross profit margin.

I tend to emphasize growth over dividends, but Kroger offers a decent 1.87% TTM dividend and the FWD is expected to weigh in at 1.91%. Walmart’s trailing dividend is .8% while the FWD is projected to be .83%. Neither company is a dividend cash machine, but KR’s is more attractive nonetheless.

Walmart

Seeking Alpha’s quant tools give WMT a hold while my fellow analysts in aggregate give Walmart a sell rating. In line with my own views, the quant tools assign an F grade for the valuation and a C- for growth. Profitability is an A+. The quant tools also give KR a hold rating, with growth getting a D mark. Valuation gets only a C- grade, but I believe that’s a bit harsh given how high PE ratios are in general right now. I still like KR as a long-term defensive stock and assign a buy rating.

Kroger

I’ll Stick With Groceries For Now

Ultimately, Walmart’s high PE ratio coupled with moderate growth and narrow profitability are the key reasons I rate WMT a sell. As for Kroger remaining a buy, I like the company’s emphasis on groceries while WMT sells everything under the sun. With many consumers struggling with financial strain and high living costs, we may see a lot of belt-tightening. This could impact groceries and people may eat less or otherwise be more frugal (say by reducing waste). However, I believe discretionary non-food spending will be impacted to a greater extent, and thus WMT will be more exposed.

During the Great Recession, many people ate out less and instead ate at home more often. I believe we’ll see a similar trend unfold right now. Amid the Great Inflation, restaurant prices have increased dramatically in many cases, and chains that don’t emphasize value, like Chipotle, have been struggling. Eating at home could become more common, and if so, Kroger and Walmart are in a good position. Kroger’s emphasis on groceries, however, should increase desirable exposure. Kroger gets almost all of its revenues from groceries, while Walmart gets only 60% of its revenues from groceries.

One thing I like about both companies is that they have strong private labels. Many Americans have been skipping brand names and buying private labels to lock in savings. About 31% of Walmart’s sales are private labels. At Kroger, private labels account for about 28% of sales. Walmart is also launching a new private label brand, “BetterGoods,” which emphasizes quality and is targeted at more affluent customers. I find this move very attractive and if WMT’s valuation metrics were in line with sector medians, I’d consider buying WMT based simply on the private label strategy.

Ultimately, recession risks are rising and I believe it’s wise to consider defensive strategies. Even if a recession doesn’t unfold, so long as consumers remain under financial pressure, staples in general strike me as attractive. Currently, consumer inflation expectations are rising and confidence is on the retreat, as shown below.

Why I Could Be Wrong

While Kroger offers a lot of value, Walmart is the value standard-bearer. As such, Kroger might even lose some customers to Walmart. Further, Walmart has seen high growth with affluent customers, who may prove more resilient to inflation in some ways. Still, even if all this turns out to be correct, I can’t justify the high valuation, although perhaps that’s a mistake on my part.

Key Take-Away: KR Offers A Safe Haven, WMT Is Simply Too Rich

As far as the business itself is concerned, Walmart offers a lot to like. However, the high PE ratio results in a sell rating for now. WMT is priced like a hot tech stock, but most of its metrics are in line with the slow-growth, thin-margin retail sector. KR’s valuation is far more attractive and I believe KR will enjoy inflation-driven tailwinds, like more people eating at home.

data.title: Dollarama: Look Through A Soft Q4
data.description:
I continue to hold my shares in Dollarama (DLMAF) and maintain a "Strong Buy" rating. It is a company I have covered before and which I find is a terrific business. The company just published its fiscal 2026 Q4 results, which fell flat, with the stock down around 8%.

It is impossible to know for sure why the price moves like this day-to-day. It's better to focus on the actual fundamental performance and any commentary around guidance - and forget the stock price for a little while. There is plenty to cover on both fronts, but first a brief overview of Dollarama itself, which is not a well-known name outside Canada.

Dollarama

I have tried to portray Dollarama as sort of an “anti-Costco (COST).” Each company’s approach to retail is about as opposite as possible, and yet, both have enjoyed fantastic results. To be clear, I am a big fan of Costco - nothing wrong with it. It is just a useful contrast, with some surprising outcomes.

As we know, Costco runs massive warehouses stocked to the ceiling with a limited set of bulky SKUs. Dollarama leases small 10,000-sqft stores in heavy traffic areas, selling everything under $5 – all singles, no bundles - and usually not much parking either!

As Costco passes all savings to customers, famously keeping gross margins in the low double digits and membership fees very steady, Dollarama is a price follower, keeping all savings from its scale and thus sporting gross margins consistently around 45%.

The results? Well, it’s a case of “The opposite of a good idea is another good idea.” Since their IPO, Dollarama has outperformed even COST on a total return basis, a fact that might surprise some people:

There are some similarities in that both are actively (and successfully) pursuing international expansion – but even there, the approach differs. Costco is going about it methodically, under its own name, while Dollarama is moving quickly under the Dollarcity joint venture and, more recently, with the small-ticket acquisition of TRS in Australia.

On capital allocation, both are excellent.

What happened in Q4?

The quarter didn't look good. SSS came in at 1.5% vs. 4.9% in the prior quarter. Earnings rose a mere 0.4%. Is this a bump in the road, or is something more sinister going on?

SSS being barely positive, it adjusts to a healthier 3.5% when considering two things: i) a calendar shift that moved half a week of Halloween sales out of Q4 and substituted a full week of pre-holiday shopping (peak season) into January, a hangover month; and ii) very poor weather during peak weeks. I usually become circumspect when retailers cite the weather, but it is difficult to deny that December was particularly harsh up north.

All in all, the slow Q4 contrasts with a strong full fiscal 2026. For the year, revenues grew 13.1% and earnings per share grew 13.7%. During the call, management noted that recent weeks with normal weather show no sign of changing consumer attitudes. Guidance for the full year was in line with past guidance and shows expected growth in SSS of 3% to 4%.

Further cementing management’s confidence, capex program remains unchanged, and the dividend was increased 13.4%, matching earnings growth, also in line with past practice. Repurchases are expected to remain the go-to method for rewarding shareholders, and 2026 saw robust buybacks.

Quite clearly, Q4 is just a bump.

International Expansion Efforts

With each earnings update, we get more color on their strategy around what has become their two main international markets (by potential, if not by financial heft – yet), Australia and Mexico.

For a complete picture, Dollarama controls Dollarcity, which operates identical stores in several LatAm countries (see below).

The only "truly overseas" operation is in Australia, a business that Dollarama acquired outright.

Australia

Dollarama is in the midst of transforming the recently purchased The Reject Store. They have outlined a four-year plan to overhaul the +400 store national chain and convert them gradually into Dollarama’s format and eventually brand.

Management guided for moderate losses this and perhaps next year on this transformation. This was to be expected, as the acquisition was more like accelerating the store footprint from which to build Dollarama. Dollarama SKUs will begin showing up in Australia later this year, while new and remodeled stores are adopting the layout, dense stocking, and general Dollarama look & feel.

I like this acquisition a lot, but the payback is more into the early 2030s. Perhaps more relevant - because of its potential size - is the other new market that opened up last year, Mexico.

Mexico

Dollarcity Mexico, 80% owned by Dollarama, entered the market last year and has opened 11 stores so far. Mexico is much larger than any other Dollarcity market, with the closest being Colombia (+400 stores already). I first covered this new market here for more context.

Management declined to provide their store target for Mexico; it's too early for that. But I expect this market will become the leader in new store openings within the next two years.

For reference, Dollarama cuts the ribbon on around 60-70 stores each year in Canada, where it already has about 1,700 stores. Dollarcity, for its part, opens 80 - 100 stores per year in LatAm (excluding Mexico). Including Mexico, Dollarcity may double Canada’s new store growth in the medium term. As such, LatAm is set to become the largest growth opportunity, which is good because I am always amazed at how many stores Canada can take.

Overall strategy considerations

Canada continues to be the most relevant market for Dollarama, and that won’t change anytime soon. In Canada, the company took the long-delayed decision to build a second logistics hub to serve the Western provinces. The ensuing double-hub system will be more resilient and more efficient, further widening the moat in a market where the next largest direct competitor is 1/6th their size.

In due course, I expect Canada store growth to slow down. This will be offset by faster growth in Dollarcity and Dollarama Australia, both of which have decades of growth to look forward to. Any slowdown in Canada will imply even more free cash flow each year—which should flow to us, likely by way of even more share buybacks.

In its home market, I see the logistics hub investment—which is massive - as the final touches on their moat. The business will remain impossible to beat at home for the foreseeable future.

Dollarama valuation

You may expect to pay a premium for results that match Costco, and that is indeed the case. The premium is, in fact, lower than Costco’s and Walmart's (WMT), which is probably fair as well.

The issue with PE is that it only considers immediate earnings. Dollarama grows at least as fast as even Costco and much faster than behemoth Walmart. So we can't just look at PE without considering relative growth paths.

Because Dollarama has higher operating leverage, the earnings growth comparison is quite telling.

Therefore, Dollarama remains my top retail stock. I continue to view it as a strong buy (even more so at the current reduced price).

Risk

With international growth come incremental exposures. I estimate (based on the past 10% incremental purchase) that Dollarcity is worth only about 20% of Dollarama. But it will likely continue to grow, both because LatAm has more white space and because Dollarama still holds call options on Dollarcity. This will introduce more currency volatility, especially around the Mexican peso and Colombian peso, which are particularly subject to wide swings.

The valuation premium is also a risk. Hiccups like this last quarter will cause price drops like we saw immediately after. No issue for long-term holders who understand the business but still uncomfortable for some. I take solace in the knowledge that more shares will get bought back at low prices.

Finally, both Mexico and Australia will require a few years of losses. In Mexico because of the need to build capacity before sales catch up and in Australia because a turnaround is needed. I feel a little better about Mexico because Dollarcity is following a template that has been successful in five out of five other greenfield LatAm markets (recognizing that Mexico is more competitive).

Takeaway

The recent earnings release was surprisingly soft for the quarter, but reassuring when taking a full-year view.

Dollarama's business model has one of the best long-term potential growth profiles in all of retail and yet remains valued as if it were to grow in line with much larger industry giants. I wouldn't go as far as stating that Dollarama is exceedingly cheap; after all, every one of the three could be overpriced to some degree. But DLMAF seems reasonably priced in relation to peers and in relation to its own historical levels.

The true beauty is in the potential for growth and future cash flow generation. Should you doubt what management intends to do with that cash, I leave you with this:

data.title: CEF Insights: ASA - Opportunity In Junior Gold Mining Companies
data.description:
CEFA:

Welcome to CEF Insights, your source for closed-end fund information and education, brought to you by the Closed-End Fund Association.

Today we are joined by Axel Merk, President and Chief Investment Officer for Merk Investments. Merk Investments is the investment advisor of ASA Gold & Precious Metals Limited (ASA).

We're so glad you could be with us.

Axel Merk:

It's great to be with you again.

CEFA:

Axel, markets have seen some volatility with shifts in U.S. trade policy, as well as geopolitical tensions. Federal deficits are challenging, but the U.S. economy has been resilient. Where do you see the investment markets currently, and what is your outlook for the rest of 2026?

Axel Merk:

Thanks for asking me when the news is changing every day, it seems. For the time being, and we're recording this three weeks into the Iran war, for the time being, the market is pricing things in as a shock. And the reason I phrase it this way is because, looking out a few months, the market seems to be pricing in some sense of normalcy, whatever that might mean. The one thing I would suggest as a word of caution as to where we're going to go the shock we're experiencing is a supply shock. And the economically sound reaction to a supply shock tends to be politically unattractive. Politicians tend to do the economically wrong thing when we are faced with a supply shock. If you go to the 1970s, for example, you have an oil shock, and you react with price controls. Not that price controls are in the cards right now, but for the time being, we have extensive volatility. There are supply issues, and if you go through the noise that's extremely volatile, yields are higher, inflation might be higher, but for the most part, markets are holding up.

CEFA:

Merk Investments has specialized expertise in the gold, precious metals, and minerals sector. When we last spoke in October 2024, gold was trading around $2,650 per ounce, and now gold trades over $5,000. What is your outlook for this sector?

Axel Merk:

The price of gold has been very strong. There have been many factors ranging from central banks buying more as the dollar has been weaponized to gold bugs not just being bugs anymore, but those fringe views coming more to the mainstream as diversification buyers buy more. Interestingly, even with the shocks we've had, and gold is off its highs, gold is still hovering around 5,000. And one of the things to keep in mind is that the gold market is much smaller than many other markets. So, it doesn't need many investors to come into the space for these prices to be as strong as they are right now.

CEFA:

Where are valuations in the precious metals mining space? And do you consider these to be attractive levels?

Axel Merk:

Given where we are on the price of gold, the miners are very, very cheap, I would argue. They've obviously come up quite substantially, welding in many times multiples in the last year and a half or so. But the margins are astounding. One of the things I'd like to mention is that in 2011, Marc Andreessen said, Software is eating the world, so to speak. Now, fast forward, AI is eating software in some ways. And the big difference is that software used to be a low barrier to entry, low cost to get into that market with high margins. But now in the current world with AI, you have huge investments, and margins are questionable. The reason I mentioned this is that we suddenly have a level playing field across industries where, on the gold mining side, of course, you have very high investments, but the margins are high—50%, 60%, 80% gross margins at times. And even with the energy shock that we are experiencing, let's assume for a moment that energy prices will remain elevated for some time, that yes, energy costs can be 20%, 25% of a producer; often they're a little bit less, but there can be this sort of magnitude. Even with that, the margins are phenomenal, and the market has been very conservative in valuing the gold in the ground. And in this context, despite the very substantial volatility we are seeing, this is actually one of the places where there might be quote-unquote value in the market.

CEFA:

Axel, we mentioned the closed-end fund you manage, symbol ASA. How is the portfolio currently positioned?

Axel Merk:

We have used ASA in a way that we believe closed-end funds are designed to be used in the sense that we have focused in on smaller, often a little bit less liquid names. And when one does that, think about it as venture capital light, that as these companies grow to the extent that they succeed—of course, not all companies will succeed—there is more than just the price of the underlying metal, gold or silver or other precious metals that they're mining, but there as these companies grow and go into production, valuations tend to be higher. And so, we are aiming at taking advantage of those regardless of the price of the metal. And the history has shown since we have taken on ASA, the management of ASA since April 2019, that that strategy has worked very, very well.

CEFA:

Looking back about two years ago in March 2024, the net asset value of ASA was about $18 per share, and the fund's discount was about 15 to 16%. Here in March 2026, the NAV per share is over $80, while the discount is roughly 13%. So, the market price performance of the fund is quite similar to the net asset value performance over time. Is it reasonable for investors to consider net asset value performance as the critical driver of shareholder returns as they evaluate their long-term expectations for the fund?

Axel Merk:

Yes, is the short answer. The current price as we're talking is about $66 a share. And the discount had been narrowing for an extended period, almost every year since we started to manage the fund. The discount had been narrowing. In recent months, the discount has been widening again a little bit. But for the most part, yes, there is a very, very strong correlation, between the NAV and the equity. And of course, we are, as the advisor, focused very much on the active management of the underlying positions. And in doing so, we are trying to add value by being invested in the sector and through the active management. I'd like to add that the mining sector is the one with the greatest dispersion of returns of any of the S&P sectors. And that's a fancy way of saying that active management really matters. The junior mining space, where we are active to a significant extent, does have slightly different risk dynamics than the producers on the large cap end. And we've been trying to add value through that. And yes, the share price tends to mimic what we do in the NAV. I'd like to add that we are very transparent. We are publishing our holdings, with some delay, on a monthly basis, and we're trying to communicate what we do, and that helps to keep the NAV and the share price somewhat aligned.

CEFA:

Axel, ASA provides investors with somewhat unique exposure to junior mining companies. How important is active management for this segment of the market, and what benefits does an allocation to junior mining companies provide in an investor's diversified portfolio?

Axel Merk:

I alluded to, kind of, that we're positioned in the junior space in part because we want to have investors participate in the accretion of the value beyond the price of the underlying commodity. The value added in a diversified portfolio is directly linked to the statistical characteristics of investing in the space. That tends to be not too closely correlated to the S&P 500 or the bond market. One of the reasons the gold space has gotten more traction is because investors are somewhat disillusioned with the bond market after decades of rising bond prices and falling yields. Not that this is a direct substitute, but investors are looking for diversification, and the uncorrelated returns that ASA provides or the space in general provides has historically been a very potentially beneficial diversifier, and being able to add value with that.

CEFA:

Axel, thank you so much for taking the time to join us today.

Axel Merk:

My pleasure.

CEFA:

We want to thank you for tuning into another CEF Insights podcast.

data.title: Caledonia Mining: Gold Prices De-Risking Growth Projects
data.description:
Caledonia Mining (CMCL) is no ordinary way to invest in gold, being a single-asset miner in Zimbabwe. With 2025 results out and big plans to expand production, CMCL is worth another look. The reward is huge down the road, but miners aren't without risks in execution.

Summary of Previous Thesis

Back in December, I covered the stock not only to catch up on Q3 result but also to consider a potential policy in Zimbabwe that was related to the higher prices of gold. Namely, it was a higher royalty tax if the price of gold were to exceed $5,000 per oz. Only a proposal at the time, I didn't think too much of it, especially as gold was still below that price.

I shifted focus to the long term. Blanket mine, their lone operating asset, was providing over $200M per year with its annual production of 75K-80K oz per share. A recently completed feasibility study had shown that Bilboes could produce an additional 200K oz per year.

This could lead to $1B in revenue and perhaps $400M to $500M in free cash flow. As the market cap was $523M at the time, this would seem like a steal once Bilboes begins production in late 2028. With three years to wait and execution risks in the meantime, I observed:

As the project is a few years away too, there's plenty of room for setbacks. Nevertheless, as CMCL appreciated and increased its dividend around the completion of capital projects at Blanket in 2021, I think we can afford to wait here.

In short, I perceived no advantage in being too early on this stock, and I maintained my Hold rating.

Q4 and Full-Year 2025 Results

Caledonia just released its unaudited 2025 financial results. Both the fourth quarter and the full year proved to be one of Caledonia's best on record.

This was in spite of lower amounts of gold produced for both periods. Even though mining capacity did not decline, management attributed the lower production to the lower-grade veins they had encountered in the year. Free cash flow still grew significantly, from $10.6M in 2024 to $62.1M in 2025.

Gold ended 2024 at about $2,600 per ounce, and its price continued to climb in 2025. There was scarcely a day last year that had a lower price than one from 2024. Management acknowledged that this was the primary reason for the superior financial results of the year.

This is in spite of the fact that operating expenses (at Blanket) were up marginally for the year. The price of gold grew so much that there were fairly insignificant factors.

Bilboes and 2026 Guidance

A significant portion of the earnings call was therefore spent not discussing the results but plans and progress on the Bilboes project. For 2026, $179.8M in capex was guided for the year. $43M is for maintenance at Blanket, while $132.1M is for Bilboes.

One will likely notice that this exceeds 2025's free cash flow, high though it was. This is because management leveraged their strong balance sheet and improvements at Blanket in previous years to secure a $150M convertible note offering. After taking into account capped calls to limit their dilutive effects, they achieved net proceeds of $130M to ensure they had the cash to begin work.

Naturally, I was wondering how the higher gold price and operating cash flow it provides might influence reliance on outside capital.

Management also provided answers to that. While they expect some of this to be self-financed, they also discussed establishing credit facilities that they can use for additional draws down the road. The longer gold prices remain high, the less they will rely on this. What this indicates is that, with every passing quarter of higher prices, Caledonia's project becomes progressively less risky.

As I was present in the call, I asked CEO Mark Learmonth if any would be used for additional solar plant, to complement the one they built a few years ago, particularly as war in the Middle East raises fuel costs. He indicated that exposure to fuel costs was low and that capital improvements were being made to improve the reliability of the local electrical grid. It's a more secondary endeavor. Throughout the presentation, it was clear that extra cash is likely to be spent on Bilboes over Blanket.

Risk Factors

While the cash flow and outlook are pretty good right now, the risk factors still loom in my mind. Mining is difficult and capital-intensive, and Caledonia is still confined to the fate of Blanket until Bilboes is ready to operate.

For example, there was a fatality in September 2025, a tragedy and a setback. Another fatality occurred just the year before. For several quarters now, management has spoken to higher labor costs as a consequence of overtime, suggesting to me that the pool of capable workers for the mine is narrow. This makes a fatality particularly injurious to the mine over its lifetime, increasing the amount of hours that remaining crew must work individually.

Of course, the weakness of the electrical grid near Blanket, which was mentioned in answering my question about solar assets, is another risk factor to consider. Mines aren't just capital-intensive; Blanket is somewhat remote and exposed to vulnerabilities like this.

Additionally, the past month has shown the price of gold decline heavily after month of climbing, almost without interruption. While it's been helping Caledonia's cash flow in real time, this also reveals how quickly and easily it could reverse. The company's ability to fund its projects becomes much harder if gold prices normalize to levels comparable to 2024 or even earlier.

Valuation

As execution is the main takeaway here, it's hard for me to count too much of Caledonia's potential upside too early. At $22 per share, the market cap is down to about $414M. This may feel like a bargain, but this also largely follows the dip in gold's price. Lower gold prices fundamentally increase the risk, as it means less self-funded growth.

The company is going to be in a wait-and-see state for quite a while. CMCL could worth over $1B in the future, potentially tripling, but setbacks could easily pull this back down to $200M. The shares are in something of a middle position still.

Conclusion

CMCL at least yields about 2.5% with its $0.14 quarterly distribution. Investors get a little something, and folks who have been in it for a while could be content to wait. Those looking to take a serious position need to consider when to do it. As it will be a few years before Bilboes is operational, a flat dividend is likely. Seeing little reason to buy right now, I maintain my Hold rating.

data.title: John Hancock Multimanager Lifestyle Conservative Portfolio Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Quarterly commentary

  • Most asset categories produced positive returns in the final three months of 2025, reflecting the favorable backdrop for the world financial markets.
  • The fund posted a gain but slightly underperformed its benchmark.
  • Asset allocation contributed to performance, while underlying manager results detracted.

Market review and outlook

Global equities registered solid gains in the fourth quarter, helping the major, broad-based indexes record their third consecutive year of double-digit returns. Performance was uneven over the first half of the quarter due to concerns that AI-related stocks were in a bubble, but the market staged an impressive rebound and went on to achieve new all-time highs by year end. A continued decline in inflation enabled the U.S. Federal Reserve to enact two quarter-point interest rate cuts, boosting sentiment. In addition, corporate earnings were robust and world economic growth remained positive. Emerging- and developed-market international equities outperformed the United States, continuing a trend that was in place for the full year. Within the U.S. market, the value style outpaced growth as investors rotated toward opportunities outside of AI-related stocks.

Global bonds logged only slightly positive total returns amid a growing consensus that most central banks were largely finished easing policy. Credit-oriented market segments continued to outperform, primarily as a result of their yield advantage.

Contributors and detractors

The majority of the benefit from allocation came from the fund's overweight in developed-market international equities and corresponding underweight in the United States. We have favored the non-U.S. markets for some time based on their attractive relative valuations, and this aspect of our positioning paid off in both the quarter and the year. An overweight in equities versus bonds was a further plus. On the other hand, an overweight in U.S. mid caps detracted. The fund's fixed-income positioning had a slightly positive effect on results, with the benefit of holding emerging-market bonds outweighing weakness in the portfolio's modest holding in U.S. Treasury STRIPS. We reduced the position in the latter category over the second half of 2025.

No single area stood out as a key driver of underperformance for the fund's active managers; instead, the shortfall was primarily a result of very small differences for a wide range of individual holdings.

Portfolio changes

We retained diversified exposure across the full spectrum of the world financial markets, while also making some modest changes on the margin to capitalize on categories where we saw attractive valuations and higher long-term return potential. We remain confident in the fund's positioning as we move into 2026, based on our belief that diversification is likely to become increasingly important. For most of the past year, investors would have been rewarded by simply holding the narrow group of mega-cap U.S. technology-related stocks that continued to dominate performance. However, we began to see this backdrop evolve somewhat over the final three months of the year with a notable broadening of leadership. While it's impossible to say if this is a sign of a larger shift, we believe investors are better served by maintaining broad-based diversification rather than relying on another year of positive relative performance for a handful of companies. We think the fund is well positioned for these circumstances through its ability to seek opportunities across a wide range of asset classes.
